normalized_variable_definitions.normalized_definition: Name or ID of the base material.
Precise chemical composition, including trace elements and units of measure, e.g., wt%, at%.
Brief paragraph describing the alloy classification.
The precise weight percentage of carbon in the alloy, which is a critical factor for avoiding intergranular corrosion or secondary phase precipitation in stainless steels.
The specific grade or processing history of the raw elements used in the melt (e.g., carbonyl nickel, electrolytic iron, spectroscopic grade molybdenum).
A classification of the alloy's cleanliness or impurity level, such as commercial-purity (induction-melted) versus high-purity (vacuum-melted) series.
The presence or qualitative amount of delta ferrite phase within the austenitic matrix, which the paper identifies as detrimental to pitting resistance.
The concentration of interstitial elements such as Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), Sulfur (S), Nitrogen (N), and Phosphorus (P), typically measured in parts per million (ppm).

Details regarding the atomic percentage (at%) of primary alloying elements (like Cr or Mo) replaced by secondary additions (like Si, Ni, Al, Nb, or Mn) to study synergistic effects on corrosion resistance.
The specific empirical formula (e.g., Cr + 3.3Mo) used to calculate the alloy's relative resistance to pitting based on its chemical composition.
The nominal thickness of the alloy sheet or plate material used for the test specimens.
A unique identifier or number assigned to a specific batch or melt of the alloy, used to distinguish between different productions of the same alloy grade.
